output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.4, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.8, "R1.5": 0.2, "R1.6": 0.3, "R1.7": 0.7}}

Summary: critique of the section; flags several claims that are overreached or inconsistent with the described method, plus missing baselines/citations.

**Major issues**

1. "First system, to our knowledge, to provide calibrated abstention behavior for general manipulation instructions." This is almost certainly wrong, or at least needs heavy qualification. KnowNo (Ren et al., 2023, "Robots That Ask For Help") already does exactly calibrated abstention/clarification for LLM-driven manipulation, using conformal prediction rather than entropy. There is also work on language-conditioned policies with uncertainty-driven clarification (e.g., "Reflect, Not Reflex" and follow-ons; Kwon et al. on LLM-grounded help-seeking). The contribution needs to be reframed as a delta against this prior art (entropy over a trajectory grid, rather than conformal sets over LLM-proposed options, etc.), not as priority.

2. Entropy-of-trajectory-distribution probably does not measure what the text claims. With a 12×12×6=864-cell action grid, two distinct semantic intents that happen to be spatially close (two adjacent blocks) will share probability mass between *adjacent grid cells anyway* — so high entropy can reflect spatial smoothing rather than semantic ambiguity. Conversely, a single unambiguous target placed at a cell boundary will smear mass across several cells, inflating entropy without any real ambiguity. The paper needs to (a) demonstrate that entropy tracks semantic ambiguity rather than grid quantization, e.g., by comparing entropy across cells around a single intended target vs. across spatially separated targets, and (b) address why mode-counting or clustering on the trajectory distribution is not the right primitive.

3. "Calibrated" claim is one-sided. tau is set so the false-execute rate is <5% on a validation split. But (i) there is no reported confidence interval / coverage guarantee that this transfers to test, and (ii) the dual error — over-abstention on unambiguous inputs — is not bounded. The 88% correct-execute on unambiguous instructions implies a 12% loss there, which is *not* "calibrated" in any standard sense (conformal, Platt, or otherwise). State this as a single operating-point selection on validation, not calibration, or report both error rates with intervals.

4. The "Generality" paragraph is contradicted by the method. The action space is a 12×12×6 grid of (x,y,θ) pick-and-place goals with linear interpolation. That cannot represent pouring, stacking sequences, non-prehensile pushes, or any instruction with a verb other than pick-and-place. Claims like "any natural-language instruction" and "arbitrary manipulation requests" should be cut. The honest statement is: tabletop pick-and-place to a discretized goal pose; abstention is over that action set.

5. Baseline framing is weak. "No-abstention" is a trivial baseline; of course it loses on ambiguous instructions. A system that always asks would also beat it. The meaningful comparison is the confidence-thresholded ablation (max-prob vs. entropy). A 14-point gap between max-prob and entropy thresholds on an 864-way distribution is surprising — these are usually strongly correlated. Either (a) the threshold tuning for the ablation was not matched (then the comparison is unfair), or (b) the gap is driven by a specific failure mode that deserves an explicit analysis figure. Also missing: an "always ask" baseline, and a comparison against KnowNo-style conformal abstention on the same scenes.

6. AmbigManip needs disclosure. 240 instruction-scene pairs is small. The text does not report: who authored the instructions, inter-annotator agreement on the unambiguous/ambiguous label, the train/val/test split (the threshold is set on "a held-out validation split" — is this disjoint from the 240?), or scene diversity. Without these, the headline numbers (91/88/27/14) are not interpretable.

7. The clarification mechanism is over-sold. The system "queries the human operator" via a "templated prompt." Templated questions cannot disambiguate cases like "the red one" when there are two red blocks of different sizes — the system has no mechanism to surface the relevant attribute. Either describe the template family and its expressive limits, or weaken the framing.

**Smaller issues**

8. The frozen 1.3B language encoder is unnamed. State the model, why it was chosen, and whether the trajectory scorer was trained on top of frozen features or jointly. Likewise the scorer architecture and training data are absent.

9. The Architecture paragraph elides how *language* gets bound to *spatial grid cells*. Concatenating a language embedding with a "visual embedding of the tabletop workspace" and predicting over 864 poses is a strong architectural choice that the section glosses over; readers cannot reconstruct or reproduce it from what is written.

10. No citations at all. At minimum, this section needs to cite the language encoder, prior LLM-clarification / abstention-in-robotics work (KnowNo and related), and any precedent for entropy-based abstention in classification (Hendrycks & Gimpel; Geifman & El-Yaniv on selective classification — which is where the term "abstention" comes from and which should be acknowledged).

11. Prose: "selectively-acting" is clunky; "selective" or "with selective execution" is cleaner. "Yielding a unified abstention policy for arbitrary manipulation requests in unstructured tabletop environments" packs three overclaims into one clause — drop or rewrite.

**Recommendations, ranked**

- Drop or sharply qualify the "first system" and "generality" claims.
- Replace "calibrated" with the precise operating-point procedure, and report both error directions with CIs.
- Add an analysis disentangling entropy-from-quantization vs entropy-from-semantic-ambiguity.
- Add an "always ask" baseline and a KnowNo-style conformal-abstention baseline, on the same scenes.
- Disclose AmbigManip construction, annotator agreement, and splits.
- Name the encoder, describe the scorer, and explain language-to-grid grounding.

**Uncertainty disclosure**: I have not verified the exact title/year of "KnowNo" or that an entropy-based selective-classification line of work uses the precise framing I named — these are recommended cross-checks, not confirmed citations. The claim that max-prob and entropy are usually strongly correlated is a general statement about discrete distributions and may not hold tightly for this particular scorer; the 14-point gap may have a legitimate explanation, but it warrants justification in the text.
